title: Dog Dragging Hind Legs: Causes, Emergency Signs & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is dragging their back legs, it is a veterinary emergency. Learn about hind limb paralysis, urgency levels, and how to get help fast.
content_html:
What is Hind Limb Paresis or Paralysis?

When a dog drags their hind legs, it is medically referred to as hind limb paresis (partial loss of movement) or paralysis (total loss of movement). This condition typically suggests a serious neurological or musculoskeletal issue, where the signals from the brain are not reaching the legs correctly. It can manifest as 'knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws), scuffing the feet, or a complete inability to stand or move the rear end.

Is It an Emergency? (Urgency Level: High)

Yes, dragging the hind legs is a high-priority emergency. Sudden loss of mobility often points to Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal cord trauma, or a vascular event like a 'spinal stroke.' These conditions are time-sensitive; the sooner a dog receives veterinary care, the higher the chance of regaining mobility. If your dog is also showing signs of intense pain, loss of bladder control, or 'deep pain' sensation, seek an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a short video of your dog attempting to move can be incredibly helpful for the triage team. It allows the veterinarian to visually assess the severity of the neurological deficit—such as whether the dog is 'ataxic' (wobbly) or completely paralyzed—before you even arrive. A photo of how your dog's paws are positioned (e.g., if they are tucked under) can also help the medical team prepare the necessary diagnostic tools for your arrival.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of a dog suddenly dragging its back legs?
faqs.answer: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is one of the most common causes, particularly in breeds with long backs like Dachshunds or French Bulldogs, where a spinal disc herniates and compresses the spinal cord.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin or ibuprofen at home for this?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medication to a dog. Some human NSAIDs are toxic to dogs, and others can interfere with the life-saving steroids or medications the vet may need to administer.

faqs.question: Is it possible for a dog to recover from hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Recovery is possible depending on the cause and how quickly treatment is initiated. Many dogs regain mobility through emergency surgery, intensive crate rest, and physical therapy.
